How to read
Composite anomaly score = |IV/HV − 1|×2 + |log10(OI-PCR)| + |log10(Vol-PCR)|×0.5 + |Change|/5 + |Corr30 − peer_mean|. Higher = more interesting.
Source: cockpit section #13-iv-hv-anomalies, live data EOD 2026-05-15.
🎭 MEME IDEA: They Don't Know — Smart money trader at the party with thought bubble: "They don't know we are paying twenty percent above average for puts." · Caption: Asymmetric trade gets built in silence.

The first observation is on options flow at the index level. Aggregate put volume across the S&P 500 options chain has been running roughly twenty percent above its trailing average for the past two weeks. Aggregate call volume is roughly fifteen percent below its trailing average for the same period. The volume put-call ratio at the index level has been trending higher even as the index has been making new highs.

That divergence is the classic signature of institutional hedging activity. When the index is rising and institutions are buying more puts than usual, you are watching an asymmetric trade get built. They are not selling their equity exposure. They are paying for protection against it.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Chad Wojak — Chad Wojak smiling, holding a SPY put contract while index makes new highs · Caption: Institutions don't pay theta for fun. They pay it when they think it's worth it.

The cost of that protection comes out of their year-end performance numbers, which is why institutions only pay for it when they think the protection is worth it.

The second observation is on the gamma exposure profile, what dealers call GEX. Dealer gamma positioning on SPY and QQQ is at the upper end of its recent range, meaning that dealers are net long gamma. The mechanical implication of net long gamma is that dealers act as a damping force on intraday volatility. When the index moves up, they sell.

When the index moves down, they buy. The dealer book quiets the tape. That is why the realized volatility on the index has been remarkably low for the past month even as the positioning extremes have built underneath. The dealer book is providing the calm.

What is interesting is the level of dealer long gamma exposure on individual mega-cap names. NVDA, AAPL, and MSFT all show dealer gamma profiles that look more neutral than the index. The dealer book that is calming the index is not equally calming the names that have driven the index. If those names get sold, the gamma damping function thins out, and individual stock volatility can spike even while the broad index seems to hold up.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Spider-Man pointing at Spider-Man — Spider-Man "SPY dealer gamma profile" pointing at Spider-Man "NVDA/AAPL/MSFT dealer gamma profile" with a Pikachu surprise face between · Caption: The damping at the index does not equal the damping at the names.

That is the mechanical setup that would produce the dispersion-closing-from-the-top scenario I described in Section 1.

The third observation is on sector-level put-call positioning. The put-call ratio on a per-sector basis has been moving in interesting directions. Technology sector put accumulation has been mild but steady for several weeks. Healthcare sector put accumulation has been very modest, which is consistent with Healthcare being already washed out and traders not bothering to hedge what they have already sold.

Consumer Discretionary put accumulation has accelerated meaningfully in the past two weeks, with the put-call ratio on XLY now in the upper quintile of its trailing 250-day distribution. That is a sector where I would have expected the marginal trade to be call buying given that Discretionary tends to lead in bullish regimes, and the fact that it is instead put buying tells you that sophisticated participants are positioning for downside in the consumer cycle. Whether that bet pays off depends on whether the broader market accommodates it, but the positioning is real and it is happening.

🎭 MEME IDEA: "When you see it" zoom — Red arrow zooming on XLY put-call ratio in the upper quintile · Caption: Sophisticated money is short the consumer cycle. The market has not noticed yet.

The fourth observation is on insider activity. Corporate insider selling has been elevated for the past four months, which is partly seasonal but is also partly cyclical. The insider sell-to-buy ratio at the S&P 500 level is running above ten to one, which puts it in the upper tier of historical readings. CEO and CFO transactions are the more meaningful subset, and those are also tilted heavily to the sell side.

The interpretation requires some care. Insider transactions can be motivated by liquidity events, by tax planning, by 10b5-1 plan executions, or by genuine information about the business. The aggregate ratio is noisy. What is more useful is to look at the cluster behavior.

Are insiders selling consistently across multiple companies in a sector? Are they selling consistently in the largest names? The answer to both questions this quarter is yes. The pattern is consistent with a market where the people who run the businesses think the valuations are stretched.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Smug Pepe — Smug Pepe with sell button labeled "10:1 sell/buy ratio at the S&P 500 level" · Caption: When the people running the businesses think the price is stretched, they don't tell you. They sell.

That is the kind of signal you weight in conjunction with positioning data, not on its own.

The fifth observation is on 13F filings, which lag by about six weeks. The most recent batch of filings from the largest hedge funds and asset managers showed two patterns worth noting. The first is that some of the most well-known long-equity managers, including the ones whose names you recognize from financial television, have been reducing their net long exposure over the past two quarters. The second is that some of the largest macro funds have been increasing their volatility long exposure through VIX futures, equity put options, and gold positions.

Neither pattern is universal. There are well-known managers who have stayed fully invested and even leveraged up. But the marginal direction in the data, especially at the long-short fund category, has been a reduction of net long exposure and a buildup of optionality on the downside. Sophisticated money is doing the same thing that the positioning indicators are telling us to do.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Hotline Bling — Drake rejecting "Shout from the rooftops that I'm short." Drake approving "Quietly trim and quietly hedge." · Caption: Sophisticated money doesn't broadcast. It positions.

They are not aggressive about being defensive. They are quietly trimming and quietly hedging.

The sixth observation is on the unusual options activity scanner, which I run nightly to flag heavy single-name flow that does not look like retail behavior. This week's flagged names include several themes worth a brief mention. The first theme is mega-cap put accumulation, especially on AMZN, GOOGL, and NVDA, where premium-weighted put activity has been running above call activity for multiple consecutive sessions. The second theme is the healthcare sector, where heavy single-name call activity has been showing up in names like LLY, NVO, and TMO, suggesting that at least some sophisticated money is positioning for a Healthcare bounce rather than treating the sector as broken.

The third theme is the materials and infrastructure space, where call accumulation has been quietly building in copper miners, electrical equipment names, and grid infrastructure companies. The interpretation across the three themes is that institutional money is hedging the most concentrated mega-cap longs while rotating into themes that are tied to the AI infrastructure build-out at the level of physical inputs rather than software outputs. That is a coherent positioning story even if no single trade screams to me.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Buy NVDA." Stage 2: "Buy SMH." Stage 3: "Buy data center REITs." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Buy the copper that powers the data centers." · Caption: Smart money rotates toward the physical inputs of the AI build.

The seventh observation is on the relationship between equity options and credit. The high-yield bond ETF, HYG, has been trading sideways for the past month while the equity index has been making new highs. The investment-grade ETF, LQD, has been similarly range-bound. The credit market is not pricing any deterioration in the corporate fundamentals or the macro backdrop.

Spreads are tight. Default expectations are low. The plumbing is clean. That is consistent with the Financial Stress reading of nineteen out of one hundred.

It is also consistent with the observation that the early phase of every market top in recent memory has occurred while credit was still calm. Credit usually lags. The equity positioning extremes lead. The credit confirmation arrives later, often only after the equity market has already begun to decline.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Old man yells at cloud (Simpsons) — Abe Simpson yelling at HYG and LQD ETFs labeled "Credit calm" · Caption: Credit always lags. It will confirm after the move. Not before.

So the absence of credit stress today should be treated as confirmation that we are early in the cycle of positioning unwinding, not as evidence that the unwinding will not happen.

Putting all seven observations together, the smart money picture this week is one of measured hedging without panic. Institutions are paying for protection. Dealers are long gamma at the index. Insiders are selling more than buying.

Macro funds are increasing volatility long exposure. Unusual options flow is concentrated in mega-cap puts and healthcare calls. Credit is calm. The story is internally consistent.

🎭 MEME IDEA: This is Fine (dog in burning room) — Dog labeled "Retail" sipping coffee in burning room while sophisticated traders in the next room are buying puts and gold · Caption: Sophisticated participants are whispering. Retail isn't listening.

Sophisticated participants are positioning for the kind of slow-moving dispersion reversal that the leading indicators suggest is in the cards. The pattern is not screaming at anyone. It is whispering. The whispers are loud enough that I am taking them seriously.

One more observation before I move on. The variance risk premium structure favors hedge buyers right now. The implied volatility on three-month S&P options is roughly two and a half points above the realized volatility on the underlying over the past month. That gap is positive but compressed relative to its historical average.

A compressed VRP means that you are not paying a large penalty for buying protection. In high-VRP regimes, when the spread between implied and realized blows out to six or seven points, hedge purchases are expensive and the smart trade is often to sell premium. In compressed VRP regimes like the current one, hedge purchases are reasonably priced and the smart trade is to be a buyer. That is one of the underlying mechanical reasons why I have been a buyer of S&P puts at ninety to one hundred and twenty days to expiration over the past two weeks.

🎭 MEME IDEA: "It's free real estate" (Eric Andre) — Trader smiling, looking at the SPY put chain: "Compressed VRP, VIX 17, SKEW 136" · Caption: This is the cheapest insurance you'll see all year. Free real estate.

The combination of low absolute VIX, compressed VRP, no SKEW pricing of tails, and elevated positioning extremes is the textbook setup for asymmetric long-protection trades. You are paying the lowest premium for protection that is most likely to be needed. The setup is rare. When it appears, you take advantage of it. That is what I am doing this week.

Section 5: Trade of the Week: ZTS

The cleanest setup I can find this week is in Zoetis, the global leader in animal health pharmaceuticals. The fundamental story is unambiguous. The technical setup is at a once-a-cycle extreme. The cross-asset context supports the thesis.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Distracted Boyfriend — Boyfriend "Market" walking past girlfriend "Zoetis at RSI 6.4" while staring at "Mag 7" · Caption: When the market sells a quality business for sector reasons and ignores the fundamentals.

The risk is bounded. The asymmetry is favorable. I am sizing this at three percent of book, which is on the higher end of my single-name sizing, because the conviction is genuinely high.

Let me walk through the thesis from the inside out.

The fundamental case for Zoetis is one of the cleaner quality stories in large-cap healthcare. The company holds dominant market share in companion animal pharmaceuticals and biologicals, which is a structurally attractive end-market. Pet ownership has been rising for two decades. Average spending per pet has been rising faster than pet ownership.

The customer relationship is sticky because veterinarians prescribe the same brands repeatedly. The product portfolio is heavily weighted toward branded biologics with multi-year patent protection. The competitive moat is operational rather than technological, which means it is more durable. The five-year revenue compound annual growth rate has been roughly eight percent.

Operating margins have been roughly thirty percent. Free cash flow conversion has been north of eighty percent. The balance sheet carries a manageable debt load with no near-term refinancing risk. None of these characteristics are signs of a broken business.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Munger photo with caption — Charlie Munger pointing finger · Caption: "30% margins. 80% FCF conversion. No refinancing risk. This is not a broken business."

The technical setup is what makes this week interesting. Zoetis closed Friday at eighty-two dollars and eighty-three cents. The fourteen-day relative strength index closed at six point four. That is not a typo.

The reading is six point four. The standard threshold for oversold conditions on RSI is below thirty. Below twenty is considered very oversold. Below ten is rare.

A reading below seven is something I see once or twice per cycle in a quality large-cap stock with a clean balance sheet. The only times in the past five years I have seen RSI readings this low on names of this quality have been March 2020, October 2022, and certain isolated single-name capitulations after company-specific bad news. The 2020 and 2022 readings were major market-wide bottoms. The single-name capitulations were usually followed by sustained rallies once the bad news was digested.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Pikachu surprised face — Pikachu wide-eyed reading "RSI 6.4 on a quality large-cap, no company-specific catalyst" · Caption: This setup happens once a cycle. Maybe twice. That's the asymmetry.

In Zoetis specifically, there has not been a single-event catalyst to explain the magnitude of the decline. The stock has been working lower for several months on a combination of soft Q1 results, currency headwinds, and broader Healthcare sector weakness. None of those factors is sufficient on its own to justify a reading of six point four on the RSI. The technical extreme is therefore disproportionate to the fundamental news flow, which is exactly the condition that historically precedes a mean-reversion rally. When the market sells a quality business for sector-rotation reasons rather than for company-specific reasons, the trade is to step in front of the rotation and buy the disconnect.

Let me put the six point four reading in some additional context. Zoetis has been a public company for thirteen years. Across thirteen years of daily RSI readings, the number of times the fourteen-day RSI has closed below ten can be counted on one hand. The last time it happened was during the March 2020 COVID panic.

The time before that was October 2014. Each of those prior episodes was followed by a sustained rally over the following one to three months. The historical sample is small. The directional bias is unambiguous.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Roll Safe (tapping head) — Smiling guy: "Small sample size means uncertainty. Uncertainty means I size at 3% instead of 5%." · Caption: The conviction is real. The sample size sets the sizing.

The cross-asset context is the third pillar of the thesis. We just walked through the sector rotation map in Section 3 and observed that Healthcare is at the bottom of the breadth rankings, with fewer than half of XLV components above their fifty-day moving average. The lagging Healthcare sector is the reason Zoetis can be at an extreme oversold RSI. The trade is therefore not just an individual stock mean-reversion.

It is also a play on the sector itself reverting from a defensive-out-of-favor extreme. We discussed at length why the dispersion between offensive and defensive sectors is at a ten-year extreme. Healthcare, alongside Utilities and Consumer Staples, is the defensive cluster that has been abandoned. If the dispersion closes through offensive selling rather than defensive buying, Healthcare may continue to lag in absolute terms while still rallying in relative terms.

If the dispersion closes through defensive buying, Healthcare leads the rotation higher. Either resolution is good for the Zoetis trade. The asymmetry across the two scenarios is what makes the setup attractive.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Two Buttons (sweating guy) — Two buttons: "Tech rolls over" and "Defensives rally." Trader smiling and pressing both. · Caption: ZTS wins on either resolution. That's the trade.

The fourth pillar is the broader screener confluence. My capitulation triplet screener, which combines low RSI, large drawdown from fifty-two-week high, and momentum z-score, has Zoetis at the top of the rankings this week. The screener has been firing on Zoetis for ten consecutive sessions, which is the kind of persistence that historically signals a setup rather than noise. The mean-reversion screener also has Zoetis at the top.

The implied-volatility-versus-realized-volatility ranker shows Zoetis options trading at a premium to realized vol, meaning that the options market is pricing some uncertainty but not catastrophic risk. The integrated read across the screener stack is high conviction.

The earnings calendar matters too. Zoetis already reported Q1 results on May second. The miss has been digested. The stock saw heavy single-day volume on the report date, and the volume profile in the days following has shown gradual stabilization.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Mr. Bean waiting — Mr. Bean watching the post-earnings volume profile dry up · Caption: Forced selling fades. Base-building begins. That's when you step in.

The post-earnings recovery pattern is the kind of behavior I look for after a stock has been washed out by a sector-led move and is beginning to find a floor. The volume on Friday closed at roughly seventy percent of the post-earnings average, which suggests that the forced selling pressure is fading. That is consistent with the technical setup transitioning from active panic into base-building.

Let me get to the specific trade structure. I am buying spot Zoetis at the eighty-three dollar range. The entry zone is eighty-two to eighty-four dollars. The stop is seventy-eight dollars, just below the recent lows and roughly six percent below entry.

The first target is ninety-two dollars, which corresponds to the fifty-day moving average and is also the previous congestion zone before the sector-led decline began. That target represents roughly ten to twelve percent upside from entry. The second target is one hundred and four dollars, which corresponds to the year-to-date midpoint and would represent a meaningful sector rotation play. That target represents roughly twenty-five percent upside from entry.

The risk-reward ratio on the first target is approximately two-to-one. The risk-reward on the second target is approximately four-to-one. The sizing is three percent of book.

🎭 MEME IDEA: ZeroHedge style "BOOM!" — Big red letters "2:1 RR on T1, 4:1 ON T2" with a stock chart and explosion · Caption: Asymmetry. The whole point.

The alternative trade structure for the more aggressive participant is options. The Zoetis options chain is currently pricing implied volatility on the at-the-money ninety-day options at roughly thirty-two percent. The historical volatility on Zoetis over the past month has been running closer to twenty-five percent. The implied-to-realized gap is positive but not extreme.

The IV rank, which measures the current implied volatility against the trailing fifty-two-week range, sits in the upper third. That means options are mildly expensive but not punitively so. The cleanest options trade given the IV setup is a long call calendar or a long call diagonal, where the structure benefits from a directional move and is helped by the IV term structure compressing. A simpler trade is the long call at the ninety dollar strike with September expiration, which costs roughly two and a half dollars.

The position pays approximately five-to-one on a move to my second target and is a complete loss below seventy-eight. The aggressive sizing on this options trade would be one percent of book. I am not personally taking the options trade. I am taking the spot.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "Long call September 90 strike." Drake approving "Spot stock with size flex." · Caption: Spot for flexibility. Options for leverage. Different tools.

The reason is that I want the position to be size-flexible, which spot allows and options do not.

What invalidates the thesis. The thesis breaks if Zoetis declines below seventy-eight dollars on a session where the broader market is also weak. That combination would tell me that the move is sector-driven rather than name-driven, and the magnitude of the move would be sufficient to take out my stop on a clean technical basis. A close below seventy-eight on company-specific bad news would also stop me out.

The risk I am taking is bounded by the stop placement. The risk I cannot eliminate is the risk of a broader market dislocation that takes Zoetis along with it. That is the risk I am paying for via the hedge book I have been building on the broader index. The hedges I described in Section 4 are partially designed to offset exactly this kind of contagion risk.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Buy stocks." Stage 2: "Buy quality stocks." Stage 3: "Buy quality stocks at RSI 6.4." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Buy quality stocks at RSI 6.4 + own SPY puts to hedge contagion." · Caption: Trade and hedge belong on the same page.

Conviction level. I rate this at seven out of ten. The seven is appropriate because the historical sample of RSI readings below ten in quality large-caps is small, and small samples introduce uncertainty. The seven is appropriate because the sector rotation thesis is consistent with the trade but not necessary for it.

The seven is appropriate because the position has a clear stop, a meaningful first target, and a reasonable upside on the second target. I would size this larger if the sample were bigger and the signal were independent of the broader thesis. The sizing reflects the conviction.

Section 5d: Four Historical Resolution Patterns

Distribution late-cycle does not resolve the same way every time. The historical record points to four distinct resolution patterns, each with different characteristics, durations, and probabilities. Naming the patterns matters because the trade structure for each is different.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Slow grind." Stage 2: "Sharp correction." Stage 3: "Sideways chop." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Late continuation." · Caption: Four historical resolutions. Three are bad for longs. Pick your defense.

Pattern 1: Slow Grind Down (40% historical probability). The most common resolution. The index drifts lower over four to nine months, with a cumulative drawdown of eight to fifteen percent. There is no single panic moment. Breadth deteriorates progressively. Mega-cap leadership rotates between names, with one breaking, then the next, until the broader index follows. Examples include the second half of 2018 and the early phase of the 2022 bear market. Trade structure: long XLU/XLP, short the leaders selectively, modest hedges. Time horizon four to nine months. Position sizing: medium.

Pattern 2: Sharp Correction (30% historical probability). A single shock catalyst converts the latent dispersion into a fast move. The drawdown is concentrated in three to eight weeks, typically twelve to twenty percent. Volatility spikes meaningfully. SKEW expands. The catalyst is rarely the one that was anticipated in advance. Examples include February 2018 Volmageddon, October 2018, and the COVID 2020 panic. Trade structure: heavy short-dated puts, VIX calls, gold long. Time horizon weeks. Position sizing: aggressive but with strict stops.

Pattern 3: Sideways Chop (20% historical probability). The index trades in a range for six to twelve months while the dispersion gradually unwinds through rotation rather than drawdown. Mega-caps cool off. The equal-weight index catches up. The gap narrows without either the leaders breaking sharply or the index making new highs. Examples include 2015 and parts of 2024. Trade structure: long the equal-weight, neutral on the index, sell volatility through covered calls. Time horizon six to twelve months. Position sizing: medium with patience.

Pattern 4: Late Continuation (10% historical probability). The dispersion continues to extend rather than resolve. The leaders push higher. The breadth deteriorates further. The eventual resolution is more violent when it arrives. Examples include the final phase of the 1999 dot-com run and certain mid-1990s episodes. Trade structure: continue long the leaders with stops, but increase the hedge size as the dispersion extends. Time horizon variable, eventually resolves to one of the other three patterns. Position sizing: directional but heavily hedged.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Coomer Wojak — Coomer staring at NVDA chart in 1999-mode · Caption: Pattern 4 is the longest party. It is also the worst hangover.

The probability split is not symmetric across the patterns. Pattern 1 (slow grind) and Pattern 2 (sharp correction) together account for seventy percent of historical outcomes. Both are unfavorable to long exposure. Pattern 3 (sideways) is neutral to mildly long-friendly through rotation. Pattern 4 (late continuation) is friendly to long for an indeterminate window before eventually rolling into one of the other three.

The trade structure I am running this week is constructed to win in patterns 1, 2, and 3, and to underperform modestly in pattern 4 with bounded downside. That is the asymmetry I am paying for.

Section 6: Other Trade Ideas

I want to lay out five additional trade ideas this week. Each one fits a different bucket. The first is a healthcare basket play that extends the ZTS thesis. The second is a multi-screener confluence in a beaten-down speculative name.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Spider-Man pointing (5 Spider-Men) — Five identical Spider-Men, labeled "ZTS extension," "Multi-screener," "Defensive rotation," "Tail hedge," "Index hedge" · Caption: One thesis. Five expressions. Different time horizons.

The third is a defensive sector rotation play through XLU. The fourth is a gold tail hedge via VGZ. The fifth is the index hedge through SPY puts that protects the broader book. Each idea is structured with a thesis, the system confluence supporting it, the levels, the risk, and the suggested sizing.

Trade Idea 2: Healthcare Basket: SYK, DHR, ABT

The Zoetis trade is the cleanest single-name play in Healthcare this week, but the breadth across the sector is so washed out that a small basket adds value relative to concentrated single-name exposure. The capitulation RSI rankings showed Stryker at thirteen point eight, Danaher at fourteen point eight, and Abbott at twenty-one. Each of these is a high-quality multinational with diversified product portfolios, strong balance sheets, and operating margins that have proven resilient across cycles. None of them has any company-specific bad news to justify the magnitude of the technical extreme. The selling has been sector-driven.

The thesis on the basket is the same as the thesis on Zoetis but with broader coverage. The Healthcare sector is at the bottom of the breadth rankings. The defensive cluster has been abandoned by sector rotation. The dispersion between offensive and defensive sectors is at a ten-year extreme.

🎭 MEME IDEA: They Don't Know — Trader at the party with thought bubble: "They don't know SYK is at RSI 13.8 and DHR at 14.8 because of sector rotation, not company news." · Caption: When sector beta sells quality, you buy quality.

When the rotation reverses, the highest-quality names in the most washed-out defensive sector will see flows first. Stryker is a leader in medical devices. Danaher is a leader in diagnostics and life sciences. Abbott is a diversified healthcare conglomerate.

The three names together provide exposure to medical devices, diagnostics, pharmaceuticals, and consumer health, which are roughly the four sub-segments of the broader healthcare opportunity. The basket diversifies single-name risk while preserving the sector thesis.

The trade structure is to allocate one percent of book to each name, for a total basket sizing of three percent. Entries are at current market: Stryker around two hundred eighty-five, Danaher around one hundred seventy-one, Abbott around eighty-four. The stops are placed five percent below entry on each name individually. The targets are the respective fifty-day moving averages, which represent the first technical level where sector rotation would be considered to have begun, and the year-to-date midpoints, which represent meaningful mean reversion. The risk-reward profiles on each name are similar at roughly two to one on first target and three to one on second target.

What invalidates the basket. The basket thesis fails if Healthcare continues to underperform for three more months without any signs of reversion. The thesis fails if the dispersion between offensive and defensive sectors widens further, which would push the high-quality healthcare names lower still. The thesis fails if a sector-wide adverse policy event, such as a major drug pricing reform proposal or an FDA enforcement action, hits the sector.

The basket sizing of three percent is designed to make the position recoverable if the thesis is wrong. The conviction is six out of ten. The conviction is lower than ZTS because the individual signals on SYK and DHR are not at the same once-a-cycle extreme as the ZTS RSI of six point four, but the basket framing provides a different kind of diversification value.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Hotline Bling — Drake rejecting "Concentrate in one healthcare name." Drake approving "Diversify across SYK + DHR + ABT." · Caption: Sector thesis, not single-name thesis. The basket structure follows the thesis.

Trade Idea 3: CORZ: Multi-Screener Confluence

Core Scientific is showing up on four of my screeners simultaneously this week. The signals firing are BOTTOM, BREAKOUT, PULLBACK, and SMART_FLOW. The combination of BOTTOM and SMART_FLOW is the highest-conviction confluence in my entire system, with a backtested Sharpe ratio of six point one seven on the relevant subset of historical trades. The combination has not produced losing setups in any of the previous occurrences. The persistence on Core Scientific has been multiple days, which adds confidence.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Spider-Man pointing (4 Spider-Men) — Four Spider-Men labeled "BOTTOM, BREAKOUT, PULLBACK, SMART_FLOW" all pointing at CORZ · Caption: Four-screener confluence. Persistence multiple days. That's a setup.

The fundamental backdrop is the Bitcoin mining infrastructure pivot. Core Scientific has been transitioning its business away from pure-play cryptocurrency mining toward providing high-density compute infrastructure for AI workloads. The capital expenditure pivot is meaningful. The execution has been gradual but visible.

The market has been skeptical, in part because the company emerged from bankruptcy not long ago and the legacy of the prior business model creates some narrative baggage. The selling has been disproportionate to the fundamental progress on the pivot, in my view, which sets up the technical mean reversion the screeners are flagging.

The trade structure is to enter at current market levels in spot. Sizing is two percent of book. The stop is placed at the recent low, providing roughly twelve percent downside protection. The first target is the fifty-day moving average, providing roughly fifteen percent upside.

The second target is the year-to-date congestion zone, providing roughly twenty-five percent upside. The risk-reward is favorable on the first target and very favorable on the second. The conviction is six out of ten. The conviction is in this range because the SMART_FLOW component of the signal is a category where backtest performance has been mixed in live trading, and the speculative nature of the underlying name limits the size I am willing to put behind it.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Roll Safe (tapping head) — Smiling guy: "Conviction 6/10 means I size at 2% not 5%. Backtest Sharpe is in-sample. Live results decide." · Caption: Discipline = sizing matches the conviction.

But the four-screener confluence with a historically validated combination is too clean to ignore entirely.

The options version of this trade is a long call at the slightly out-of-the-money strike with September expiration. The implied volatility is elevated, which makes the long call expensive in absolute terms, but the upside-to-premium ratio remains attractive given the upside target. If the move plays out cleanly, the call returns three to four times premium. The aggressive sizing is half a percent of book on the call. I am taking the spot rather than the call because I want the position to participate in any extended move rather than being constrained by the September expiration window.

What invalidates the thesis. The thesis fails if Core Scientific declines below the recent low on heavy volume, which would invalidate the technical setup. The thesis fails if the broader cryptocurrency mining sector breaks down in a way that drags the name lower regardless of the AI pivot story. The thesis fails if the SMART_FLOW signal turns out to have been hedging rather than directional, which would mean the institutional flow was buying puts against an existing long rather than building a new long position.

The risk is bounded by the stop. The thesis review point is two weeks out, at which point the signal should have produced enough of a move to validate or invalidate the setup.

Trade Idea 4: XLU Long: Defensive Rotation Play

The Utilities sector trade is the cleanest expression of the broader rotation thesis. If the dispersion between offensive and defensive sectors is going to close, Utilities is the most likely beneficiary on the defensive side. The XLK to XLU ratio is at a ten-year extreme. The Tech-over-Utilities trade has been crowded for years. The unwinding of that crowded trade benefits Utilities by definition, even if Utilities does nothing in absolute terms.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Bull/Bear cartoon — Cartoon bull labeled "Crowded Tech long" sweating, cartoon bear labeled "XLU" smiling on the sidelines · Caption: Crowded trade unwinds. Defensive sector gets the flow.

The fundamental backdrop is favorable. The interest rate cycle has stabilized. The yield curve is no longer inverted. Utilities pay dividend yields that compare well to short-duration Treasuries.

The earnings profiles of the largest utility companies are stable, with regulated rate base growth providing visibility. The AI infrastructure build-out has produced surprising tailwinds for utility names that supply power to data centers, with several large utilities reporting meaningful new customer contracts. The narrative around utilities is not as exciting as the narrative around AI software, but the underlying numbers are working.

The technical setup is unusual. The XLU is currently trading within roughly one percent of its fifty-two-week high. Most defensive plays are entered after a meaningful drawdown, when the defensive sector has been beaten down and the entry is contrarian. This trade is different.

It is entered close to the high. The reason is that the relative position of XLU to XLK has the relative space to revert without XLU needing to decline. If XLK declines and XLU holds, the ratio compresses meaningfully. The trade therefore does not require XLU to make new highs.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Buy weak sector low." Stage 2: "Buy weak sector mid." Stage 3: "Buy weak sector at relative-extreme." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Buy the defensive close to its high because the offensive has to mean-revert." · Caption: The relative trade does not require absolute weakness.

It only requires XLU to hold its existing level while XLK gives back some of the dispersion premium.

The trade structure is to buy XLU at current levels with a sizing of two percent of book. The stop is placed roughly five percent below entry, which would correspond to a clean breakdown from the recent range. The first target is unspecified on the absolute level. The trade is structured as a long position to be held until either the dispersion closes by ten percent on the XLK over XLU ratio, or until the stop is hit.

The expected hold period is two to four months. The conviction is six out of ten.

The hedge interpretation. The XLU long is also a hedge against the overall book. If the broader market sells off, XLU has historically held up better than the cap-weighted S&P. The defensive characteristics of the sector provide a degree of downside protection even if the relative trade does not close as quickly as expected. The trade has dual utility as both an absolute long and a relative-value spread component.

Trade Idea 5: VGZ: Gold Tail Hedge

Vista Gold is a small-cap gold exposure that I keep in the book as a tail hedge by design. The thesis is not that gold is going to rally next week. The thesis is that gold provides asymmetric upside in scenarios where the broader market experiences a dislocation, particularly a dislocation tied to currency, geopolitics, or financial system stress. The cost of holding the position is the slow time decay of the underlying gold beta, which in calm regimes can be small or even negative. The benefit is the convex payoff in stress scenarios.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Mr. Bean waiting — Mr. Bean sitting on a bench labeled "VGZ tail hedge," checking his watch · Caption: Insurance pays slow. Then it pays fast.

The fundamental backdrop is mixed. Gold has been trading sideways near recent highs for the past several months. The dollar has been range-bound. Real interest rates are positive but not aggressively so.

The macro backdrop is not screaming for gold to outperform. What the backdrop does support is gold maintaining its bid in scenarios of equity market stress, because the cross-asset correlation properties of gold tend to flip negative against equities in periods of risk-off.

The trade structure is to hold the existing position rather than to add. Sizing is one and a half percent of book and has been roughly that for several months. There is no stop on this position because the design is to hold through volatility and let the convex payoff emerge in stress scenarios. The first target is unspecified.

The position is treated as a long-duration insurance policy. The expected hold period is multi-quarter to multi-year. The conviction on the absolute direction is low. The conviction on the hedge utility is high.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "Trade VGZ for direction." Drake approving "Hold VGZ for convexity." · Caption: Different instrument, different role.

VGZ versus GLD versus GDX. The reason I am holding VGZ rather than GLD or GDX is that the small-cap exposure provides higher beta to the underlying gold price, which is what I want from a tail hedge. The trade-off is that VGZ has higher idiosyncratic risk than GLD and higher operational risk than GDX. The sizing is small enough that the idiosyncratic risk is acceptable.

The reader who wants cleaner gold exposure should use GLD or GDX, which sacrifice some convexity for lower idiosyncratic risk. The strategic role in the book is the same.

Trade Idea 6: SPY Puts: Index Hedge

The hedge book on the broader index is the most important risk-management position in my portfolio this week. The thesis on the positioning extremes from Section 1 implies that the broader market faces a measurable probability of a meaningful drawdown in the next sixty to one hundred and twenty days. The cost of insuring against that drawdown is currently low, as I detailed in the variance risk premium discussion in Section 4. The asymmetric trade is to buy the insurance now and let it run.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Smug Pepe — Smug Pepe holding a SPY 90-day put labeled "6-7% OTM" · Caption: When you can buy insurance for theta peanuts, you do.

The trade structure is to hold long positions in SPY put options at ninety to one hundred and twenty days to expiration, with strikes roughly six to seven percent below the current spot. The total sizing on the hedge book is approximately one and a half percent of book as measured by premium paid. The maximum loss is the premium itself, which is fully expensed if the puts expire worthless. The maximum gain is delta-driven and grows as the underlying declines, with a meaningful kink in the payoff if the market drops below the strike.

The reason for the duration choice. Ninety to one hundred and twenty days is the duration that captures the historical window in which positioning extremes resolve, which is the window the xMarketTopMas backtest validates. Shorter-duration puts have higher theta decay and require precise timing. Longer-duration puts have lower decay but also require more capital to maintain the same delta exposure. The ninety to one hundred and twenty day window is the practical sweet spot for the hedge.

The reason for the strike choice. Six to seven percent below spot puts the strike close to the expected drawdown level if the positioning thesis plays out modestly, and well above the level the strike would need to reach to provide meaningful protection against a deeper drawdown. Deeper out-of-the-money strikes are cheaper but pay only on tail scenarios. At-the-money strikes are expensive and pay too aggressively to be efficient. The six to seven percent range is the cost-efficiency optimum for the kind of asymmetric protection the broader thesis demands.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Two Buttons (sweating guy) — Two buttons: "5% OTM (sharp move)" and "6-7% OTM (typical drawdown)." Trader confidently picks 6-7%. · Caption: Strike selection is not random. It's a model.

Conviction. The conviction on the hedge is eight out of ten. The conviction is high because the cost is low, the asymmetry is favorable, and the underlying thesis is supported by multiple independent positioning indicators. The conviction is not ten because the timing of the resolution is uncertain and the hedge could expire worthless if the resolution arrives after one hundred and twenty days. The active management of the hedge book involves rolling expiring positions into new positions if the positioning extremes persist, which is the action plan if the thesis remains valid into August.

Brief Mention: BUR Continuation

The Burford Capital position from last week's book is being maintained at a reduced size. The YPF appeal remains the idiosyncratic catalyst. The story has been working slowly. The position is up roughly twenty-five percent from entry.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Chad Wojak — Chad smiling, trimming a BUR position from 2.5% to 1% while keeping the YPF appeal optionality · Caption: Take some profit. Let the rest run with no risk.

The conviction is decreasing slightly after a soft earnings print, but the catalyst structure is independent of the broader macro thesis, which is the reason I am keeping the residual position. Sizing is approximately one percent of book, reduced from the previous two and a half. The stop is moved up to break-even. The thesis is allowed to run with a position size that is small enough to not move the book if it fails.

Trade Selection Summary

Six trade ideas. ZTS at three percent (highest conviction). Healthcare basket of SYK, DHR, ABT at one percent each (sector confluence). CORZ at two percent (multi-screener confluence).

XLU at two percent (dispersion rotation). VGZ at one and a half percent (tail hedge, maintained). SPY puts at one and a half percent premium (broad index hedge). BUR at one percent (idiosyncratic catalyst, reduced).

Total deployed sizing: approximately fourteen percent of book in active positions, plus the gold and hedge components. The remaining sixty plus percent is cash. The book is positioned for the rotation thesis while maintaining meaningful liquidity to add if the thesis plays out and creates better entry points on the long side.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Buffett photo with caption — Buffett holding a coke, looking calm · Caption: "Cash is not zero. Cash is option value. Especially when everyone else is fully invested."

Section 5b: Additional High-Conviction Trade, APTV

Aptiv is the second highest-conviction trade in the book this week. The setup is conceptually similar to Zoetis but with different drivers. APTV closed Friday at $57.94.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Spider-Man pointing at Spider-Man — Spider-Man "BOTTOM signal" pointing at Spider-Man "KERBEROS_RSI signal" both labeled "Fired 10 consecutive sessions" · Caption: Persistence is rare. Two screeners firing daily for two weeks is a setup.

The signal stack is unusually clean. BOTTOM and KERBEROS_RSI have fired on APTV for ten consecutive sessions, every single trading day from late April through May 8. That kind of persistence is rare. The two screeners measure different things. BOTTOM looks for capitulation washouts.

KERBEROS_RSI looks for momentum exhaustion in quality names. When both fire daily for two weeks straight on the same ticker, you have a setup, not a noise event.

A third signal fired the day after the earnings drop. PEAD, the post-earnings drift screener, triggered on May 6. The pattern is the textbook recovery bounce after a sharp post-earnings move. The two persistence signals do most of the work. The PEAD confirmation seals it.

A fourth signal joined on Friday. ALTMAN, the financial health screen, flagged APTV on May 8, which is unusual for a name that has just dropped twelve percent on earnings. The ALTMAN screen filters for balance sheet quality and cash flow sustainability. Its trigger here means the underlying business profile is intact even after the post-earnings reset.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "Balance sheet broken." Drake approving "Balance sheet intact, technical washout, four-screener confluence." · Caption: When ALTMAN flags after a -12% earnings drop, the fundamentals say buy.

Aptiv reported Q1 before the open on Tuesday May 5. Revenue $5.1 billion (+5% YoY). Adjusted EPS $1.71. Adjusted EBITDA $752 million.

Management called it a record first quarter. The headline numbers were strong. The stock still saw a sharp post-earnings sell-off driven by guidance digestion and the post-Versigent spin-off reset that completed April 1.

The technical setup. APTV is trying to build a base in the high-fifties near the lower end of its multi-year range. The 2025 capitulation low was $47.19, set on April 11, 2025. The 2026 low so far is $52.38, set intraday on May 5 during the earnings drop. Medium-term, the trend is rebuilding. Long-term, it stays broken. The risk-reward is favorable because the stop sits just below the post-earnings low and the target sits at the prior April congestion zone.

🎭 MEME IDEA: ZeroHedge "BOOM!" — Big red letters "Stop $54, T1 $62, T2 $68" over APTV chart · Caption: Clear stop. Clear target. No drama.

The trade structure. Entry zone $57 to $58. Stop $54, just under the post-earnings low. Target 1: $62 (prior April congestion). Target 2: $68 (gap-fill from the April 1 spin-off break). Sizing 2.5% of book.

Conviction 7 of 10. The position is structured as spot rather than options because implied volatility is elevated post-earnings and the hold period is open-ended.

What invalidates the thesis. APTV breaks $54 on a session with broader risk-off. The mean-reversion thesis fails if the broader market dispersion accelerates and drags quality cyclical names lower regardless of company-specific fundamentals.

Section 5c: Three Tactical Lottery Plays Aligned With The Thesis

These three trades are structured optionality on the distribution late-cycle thesis itself. They are not random speculative names. Each one is designed to monetize a specific dimension of the dispersion resolution. The expected hit rate on any one of them is below fifty percent.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "YOLO calls." Stage 2: "OTM calls on AI stock." Stage 3: "OTM SPY puts." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Three structured lotteries, each sized 0.5%, each aligned with the thesis." · Caption: Lottery plays with discipline. Asymmetry is the whole point.

The expected payoff if the thesis plays out is multi-bagger. The asymmetry is the entire reason they fit.

Sizing rule: maximum 0.5% of book per leg, total basket maximum 1.5%. Each trade is structured to lose all of its premium without changing the broader portfolio trajectory. Each trade has the kind of upside that justifies the small allocation.

Lottery Play 1: VIX Calls, 30-DTE, Strike 22

The single cleanest expression of the distribution late-cycle thesis is to be long volatility. The VIX Spike Detector on my dashboard sits at Tier 2, MEDIUM CONVICTION that the VIX exceeds 25 within the next 120 trading days. The conditional probability is roughly sixty-five percent against a base rate of thirty-five percent. The asymmetric trade is to position for the spike now, when the VIX is in the bottom one percent of its trailing thirty-day range and call options are correspondingly cheap.

Trade structure. Long VIX calls at the 22 strike with 30 days to expiration. The current VIX is 17.08, which makes the 22 strike roughly 29% out-of-the-money. The premium is therefore small. Sizing 0.5% of book in premium.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Pepe smug — Smug Pepe holding a VIX 22 call ticket with a knowing grin · Caption: 29% OTM at VIX 17. Premium peanuts. Payoff unbounded.

The maximum loss is the entire premium if VIX stays below 22 through expiration. The maximum gain is unbounded and grows non-linearly above 22.

Why 30-DTE rather than 90-DTE. The shorter duration captures the kink in the convexity profile, which is what you want for a tactical spike trade. Longer-dated VIX calls have meaningfully less gamma per dollar of premium and decay slower but capture spike less efficiently. The 30-day trade is the duration where vega and gamma trade off optimally for a spike scenario.

Why 22 strike rather than ATM. The 22 strike sits roughly at the level where the VIX has historically broken on the most recent regime transitions. ATM calls are too sensitive to small VIX moves and require precise timing. OTM calls below 22 give cheaper premium for a less likely outcome but suffer if the spike is intermediate. The 22 strike is the cost-efficiency sweet spot.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Roll Safe (tapping head) — Smiling guy: "You can't lose to theta if your sizing is 0.5%." · Caption: Lottery discipline means sizing first, conviction second.

What invalidates. The thesis fails if the VIX trades sideways below 20 for the duration. The premium decays. The loss is bounded by the sizing.

Lottery Play 2: XLU Calls, 60-DTE, Slightly OTM

If the dispersion between Tech and Utilities closes from the top, XLU does not need to rally to make the relative trade work. But if the rotation is sharp enough, XLU itself catches a meaningful bid as institutional flow rotates from offensive into defensive. The XLK over XLU ratio sits at a ten-year high. The asymmetry is that XLU has roughly twenty percent of upside before its absolute valuation looks stretched, and the rotation could easily push it there if the dispersion resolves quickly.

Trade structure. Long XLU calls at a strike roughly 3 to 5% above current spot, with 60 days to expiration. Current XLU is 47.15. Strike around 49 to 50. Sizing 0.5% of book in premium.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Bull cartoon — Cartoon bull labeled "XLU OTM call" charging up · Caption: Defensive sector with leveraged upside if the rotation arrives. Convex on speed.

The maximum loss is the premium if XLU stays flat or declines. The maximum gain is leveraged to the upside if the rotation arrives.

Why 60-DTE rather than 30-DTE. Sector rotations historically take longer to play out than VIX spikes. The 60-day duration gives the trade more room to develop while still maintaining acceptable theta. Longer durations dilute the gamma efficiency without proportionate benefit.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Mr. Bean waiting — Mr. Bean tapping his watch, 60-day timer in the background · Caption: Sector rotations play out slow. Pick the duration that matches the velocity.

Why slightly OTM rather than ATM. ATM XLU calls are expensive in absolute terms because XLU options have moderate implied volatility. OTM gives cheaper premium for a meaningful upside payoff. The strike at 49 to 50 puts the breakeven roughly 4 to 6% above spot, which is achievable in any meaningful sector rotation.

What invalidates. The thesis fails if the dispersion does not close in the duration window. The thesis fails if the rotation goes the other way and offensive sectors continue to outperform. The premium decays. The loss is bounded.

Lottery Play 3: SPY OTM Puts, 30-DTE, Strike 5% Below Spot

The third tactical lottery is the catastrophe insurance trade. Buying short-dated SPY puts at 5% out-of-the-money is the cheapest insurance against a sharp drawdown that I can find in this regime. The VIX is at 17. SKEW is at 136. Premium is depressed across the curve. If the distribution late-cycle thesis resolves through a sharp move rather than a slow grind, these puts return five to ten times premium in a few sessions.

🎭 MEME IDEA: ZeroHedge style "OH NO" — Big bold "5x-10x return in days if the sharp resolution arrives" over SPY chart with a sudden drop · Caption: Premium peanuts. Payoff parabolic. The textbook lottery.

Trade structure. Long SPY puts at a strike 5% below current spot (current SPY 737.62, strike around 700), with 30 days to expiration. Sizing 0.5% of book in premium. Maximum loss is the premium if SPY does not break 700 within 30 days. Maximum gain is leveraged delta as SPY trades through the strike.

Why 30-DTE rather than 90-DTE. The 30-day duration concentrates the convexity if the move arrives soon. The 90-day puts in the broader hedge book, sized at 1.5% of premium, are the strategic insurance. The 30-day puts here are the tactical lottery on a sharp resolution.

Why 5% OTM rather than ATM. ATM puts are expensive and pay too aggressively to be efficient at 30-DTE. The 5% OTM strike captures the meaningful drawdown scenarios while keeping the premium small enough that the position is recoverable on a complete loss.

Why this is different from the strategic SPY put hedge in Section 6. The strategic hedge is sized 1.5% of premium across 90-120 DTE strikes 6-7% OTM. It is designed to cover the expected window for the full thesis to play out. The tactical lottery here is sized 0.5% of premium at 30-DTE 5% OTM. It is designed to monetize a sharp resolution if it arrives in the next month, while accepting that the premium decays to zero if the resolution is slower.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "One big SPY put bet." Drake approving "Two layers: strategic 90-120D + tactical 30D." · Caption: Different durations for different scenarios. Layered protection.

What invalidates. The thesis fails if SPY trades sideways or higher for 30 days. The premium decays. The loss is bounded.

Tactical Lottery Synthesis

Three lottery plays at 0.5% each, total basket 1.5% of book. All three are aligned with the distribution late-cycle thesis, which means the basket is correlated to the strategic positioning rather than orthogonal to it. That is intentional. The basket monetizes specific dimensions of the same thesis: VIX calls capture the volatility expansion, XLU calls capture the rotation into defensives, SPY OTM puts capture the sharp drawdown scenario. If the thesis plays out, two or three of the three pay off. If the thesis does not play out, all three lose their premium and the cost is recoverable.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Three correlated lotteries on the same thesis sized to lose all premium without changing the book." · Caption: Convex on direction. Convex on speed. Total bet 1.5%.

The reason for tactical lottery alongside strategic hedge book. The strategic hedges (SPY puts 90-120 DTE 6-7% OTM, sized 1.5% of premium) cover the expected window. The tactical lottery (these three trades, 30-60 DTE, sized 1.5% combined) covers the sharp-move scenario where the thesis resolves faster than the strategic window. The two together produce a payoff profile that is convex on speed of resolution as well as on direction. That kind of convexity is the entire point of running a defensive book through a positioning extreme.

Section 7b: Why Maintained Positions Are Maintained

A portfolio is the sum of two decisions repeated daily: what to add and what to keep. Most newsletters discuss the additions. Few discuss the keeps. The keeps are often the most important decisions in a book because they reflect conviction that has been tested over multiple weeks rather than conviction that is fresh and unproven.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "What to add this week." Drake approving "What to keep this week." · Caption: Most newsletters discuss the buys. The real decision is what to hold.

Why VGZ stays in the book. Vista Gold is a small-cap gold producer. The position is held as a tail hedge by design. It is not held because gold is going to outperform next month. It is held because in scenarios where the broader market experiences a dislocation tied to currency stress, geopolitical shock, or financial system pressure, gold typically catches a bid that is independent of the equity market. The cost of holding the position is roughly negative two percent year-to-date, which is the slow time decay of carrying tail-hedge exposure during a calm regime. The benefit emerges asymmetrically when the regime breaks. The conviction on the absolute direction is low. The conviction on the hedge utility is high. The role in the book is insurance, not directional beta.

Why BUR stays in the book. Burford Capital is a litigation finance specialist. The thesis when entered was the YPF appeal, an idiosyncratic catalyst tied to a specific legal proceeding that has multi-billion dollar payoff potential if it resolves favorably. The catalyst is independent of broader macro conditions, which is the key feature. A position that does not depend on the macro thesis adds diversification value to a book that is otherwise heavily exposed to the macro thesis.

The position is up roughly 25% from entry. The size has been reduced from 2.5% to 1% over the past two weeks as profit-taking happened. The remaining 1% is allowed to run on the remaining catalyst optionality. The stop is moved to break-even, which means the worst case on the remaining position is a complete return of the realized gains.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Smug Pepe — Smug Pepe smiling at a BUR position chart up 25% · Caption: Trim winners. Move stop to break-even. Sleep at night.

The expected case is that the YPF resolution adds another leg higher to the position over the next several months. The conviction is medium because legal catalysts are inherently uncertain in timing.

Why the SPY put hedge book stays in place. The hedge book is structurally different from the long positions. It is not held because puts will pay off next week. It is held because the cost of insurance during calm regimes is meaningfully cheaper than the cost during stressed regimes. Buying hedges when VIX is at 17 is cheap. Buying hedges when VIX is at 27 is expensive. The position is currently down roughly ten percent of premium paid, which is the carrying cost of unused insurance. The cost will be recovered many times over if the broader thesis plays out. The cost will be lost in full if the thesis is wrong. The asymmetry favors holding through the decay.

The general principle across maintained positions. Each maintained position represents a different kind of optionality. VGZ optionality on a regime break. BUR optionality on a legal catalyst. SPY puts optionality on equity dispersion resolving from the top. None of them require the central thesis to play out exactly as forecast. Each one adds payoff structure to a book that would otherwise be heavily concentrated in either the cash position or the freshly-added long positions in healthcare and the trade ideas list. Diversification of payoff structure is more valuable than diversification of single-name exposure in a regime where positioning is the dominant risk factor.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Diversify across stocks." Stage 2: "Diversify across sectors." Stage 3: "Diversify across asset classes." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "Diversify across payoff structures." · Caption: The third axis of diversification is the one nobody teaches.

Section 7: Portfolio Performance and Positioning

I share entry levels, stops, and targets. I do not take every position I flag, and I do not always size positions identically to what the newsletter suggests. The portfolio described here is a model portfolio that is updated weekly at Friday's close. My personal positions may differ in timing and sizing. The reason I publish the model portfolio is to give you a transparent view of what the system is actually recommending and how the recommendations evolve over time, rather than just publishing trade ideas in isolation.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Buffett photo with caption — Buffett holding cherry coke, calm · Caption: "Transparency is free. Hide nothing. Track everything."

The book this week sits at approximately sixty percent cash and forty percent deployed across active positions. The cash position is the largest it has been at any point this year, and the cash growth has been progressive over the past three weeks as I have trimmed winners and reduced exposure in names where the conviction has decayed. The deployed positions break down as follows.

The Zoetis position is being initiated this week at three percent of book. The thesis was detailed in Section 5. The position is freshly added and has no profit and loss to report. The entry is at Friday's close.

The stop is at seventy-eight dollars. The targets are at ninety-two and one hundred and four dollars. The position is sized to be the highest-conviction single-name play in the book.

The Healthcare basket of Stryker, Danaher, and Abbott is being initiated this week at one percent each, for a total basket sizing of three percent. The basket entry is at Friday's close. Stops on each name are five percent below entry. Targets on each name are the respective fifty-day moving averages and year-to-date midpoints.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Distracted Boyfriend — Boyfriend "Single-name exposure" walking past girlfriend "Sector thesis" while staring at "ZTS" · Caption: Basket sizing follows the thesis, not the single-name conviction.

The basket sizing is structured to provide sector exposure beyond the single-name Zoetis trade while diversifying name-specific risk. The basket is conceptually one position even though it is three line items.

The Core Scientific position is being initiated this week at two percent of book. The thesis was detailed in Section 6. The position is freshly added. The stop is at the recent low.

The first target is the fifty-day moving average. The second target is the year-to-date congestion zone. The trade is structured as a swing position with an expected hold period of two to six weeks.

The XLU position is being initiated this week at two percent of book. The thesis was detailed in Section 6. The position is structured as a relative-value spread component as well as an absolute long. The stop is five percent below entry.

The trade has no defined target on the absolute level. The exit condition is either a closing of the XLK over XLU ratio by ten percent or a hit on the absolute stop. The expected hold period is two to four months.

The Vista Gold position is being maintained at one and a half percent of book. The position has been roughly that size for several months. The position is structured as a tail hedge by design and is held through volatility rather than traded actively. The current profit and loss on the position is roughly negative two percent from entry.

The negative carry is the cost of the insurance. The position is doing exactly what it is supposed to do, which is to be there when the rest of the book needs it.

The SPY put hedge is being maintained at one and a half percent of book as measured by premium paid. The position consists of multiple expiration cycles, with the bulk of the exposure concentrated in the ninety to one hundred and twenty day window. The strikes are six to seven percent below spot. The current profit and loss on the hedge is approximately negative ten percent of premium, reflecting time decay over the past two weeks.

The hedge is doing what hedges do when the underlying does not move. The decay is the cost of carrying the protection until the protection is needed.

The Burford Capital position is being maintained at one percent of book, reduced from two and a half percent two weeks ago. The reduction was a tactical trim to lock in some profit while keeping the YPF appeal catalyst exposure. The current profit and loss on the remaining position is approximately positive twenty-five percent from entry. The stop has been moved to break-even. The position will be allowed to run with low downside risk and capped upside given the smaller sizing.

The IREN position from two weeks ago was exited last week at approximately sixty dollars from an entry at forty-five and sixty-six. The realized profit was thirty-three percent in eight trading days. The exit was triggered by my screener confluence weakening and the price reaching the resistance level I had identified pre-trade. The exit was clean and the trade is closed.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Chad Wojak — Chad smiling holding "+33% in 8 days IREN closed" sign · Caption: When the signal weakens AND price hits resistance, you take the win.

The other closed positions from the prior eight weeks were exited at strength to build the current cash position. CORT was closed at fifty-one and a quarter for plus thirty-nine percent from entry. Jazz Pharmaceuticals was closed at two hundred and two seventy-two for plus twenty percent. Jefferies was closed at forty-nine and twenty-nine for plus twenty-three percent.

Each of these exits was triggered by either the achievement of the original target or by the screener confluence weakening on the name. The total realized profit from the closed-position cohort over the past eight weeks is approximately seven percent on the book, which compares favorably to the broader index performance over the same period.

The unrealized positions that closed at flat or modestly negative include LULU, which was stopped out at one hundred forty after an entry zone of one hundred forty-three to one hundred forty-eight, and VKTX, which is currently sitting at minus nineteen percent without a specific catalyst. The LULU stop did its job and the position is fully resolved. The VKTX position is being held with reduced sizing because the original thesis on weight-loss drug exposure has not played out as expected and the technical setup has been weak. The position is approaching a decision point.

If the name does not recover meaningfully in the next two weeks, the position will be closed for a loss. The hold-and-hope thesis is one I generally avoid, and the VKTX position is currently in danger of becoming that kind of position. The discipline is to close it cleanly rather than to extend the thesis indefinitely.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Cope Pepe (sad Pepe with tear) — Cope Pepe holding a VKTX chart at -19% with thought bubble "but the thesis though" · Caption: Hold-and-hope is not a thesis. Cut. Move on.

The book composition this week therefore reads as follows. Cash, sixty percent. Long positions, thirteen and a half percent (ZTS, SYK, DHR, ABT, CORZ, XLU, BUR). Hedge positions, three percent (VGZ, SPY puts).

VKTX residual, one percent. Total deployed, approximately seventeen and a half percent. The cash position is the dominant component by design. The cash is dry powder waiting for the rotation to create entry points on the long side that are better than what is currently available.

If the rotation produces a meaningful drawdown in the broader market over the next ninety days, the cash will be deployed into single-name long positions in the most washed-out quality names. If the rotation does not arrive, the cost of holding cash is the opportunity cost of not participating in a rally that continues higher. The asymmetry of the decision favors holding cash given the positioning extremes that frame the broader thesis.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Mr. Bean waiting — Mr. Bean on a bench labeled "60% cash, 0 rush" · Caption: Holding cash at a positioning extreme is not opportunity cost. It's the position.

The performance metrics for the model portfolio year-to-date are as follows. Year-to-date return is approximately plus twelve percent. The relevant benchmark, the S&P 500, is approximately plus fifteen percent year-to-date. The relative underperformance is roughly three percentage points.

The volatility of the portfolio year-to-date is approximately ten percent annualized, which compares to the S&P 500 at roughly fifteen percent. The Sharpe ratio year-to-date is approximately one point two. The maximum drawdown year-to-date is approximately negative four percent, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 drawdown of negative eleven percent earlier in the year.

The interpretation of the relative performance numbers is straightforward. The portfolio has captured roughly eighty percent of the index upside while running with two-thirds of the volatility and one-third of the drawdown. That profile is consistent with the philosophy of the system, which prioritizes risk-adjusted returns over absolute returns. The cost of running this way is that the portfolio underperforms in straight bull markets where everything goes up and risk-management adds friction.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Galaxy Brain (4 stages) — Stage 1: "Beat the index." Stage 2: "Beat the index every year." Stage 3: "Beat the index on a risk-adjusted basis." Stage 4 (glowing brain): "80% upside, 2/3 vol, 1/3 drawdown." · Caption: The numbers no one shouts about.

The benefit is that the portfolio outperforms in drawdowns where risk-management protects capital. The aggregate result over a full cycle has historically been favorable, but it requires the discipline to underperform during the bull phase and the conviction to deploy aggressively during the drawdown phase.

The current positioning is therefore one of patience. The discipline to remain heavily in cash while the index makes new highs is the hard part of running this kind of system. The temptation to chase the rally is always present. The historical record on chasing rallies after positioning extremes have been reached is not favorable. The decision to hold cash is the correct decision even when it feels uncomfortable.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Doomer Wojak — Doomer Wojak smoking, looking at the index making new highs · Caption: The discomfort of cash at the top is the cost of avoiding the drawdown.

One last observation on the portfolio. The total Healthcare exposure across ZTS, SYK, DHR, and ABT is six percent. That is the largest sector concentration in the book. The exposure is intentional.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Spider-Man pointing (4 Spider-Men) — Four Spider-Men labeled "ZTS, SYK, DHR, ABT" all pointing at "Healthcare 6% sector concentration" · Caption: The biggest sector tilt is by design. Build conviction. Size accordingly.

It is built on the thesis that Healthcare is the most washed-out defensive sector and that the rotation reversal benefits the highest-quality names in this sector first. The concentration is justified by the sector signal strength and the breadth of the screener evidence. If the thesis is wrong, the basket sizing limits the damage. If the thesis is right, the basket participates broadly across the sector.

The trade-off is acceptable.

Section 8: What I'm Watching

The week ahead has several specific events and technical thresholds that will determine whether the broader thesis plays out or needs to be modified. Let me walk through them in order of importance.

06
Kerberos macro · plumbing
Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4) OBSERVATION
25.64 LOW
STLFSI4 = -0.76 · score 0-100 = 26
FSI 26/100 (LOW). Plumbing financière calme — risk-on tailwind.
How to read
St Louis Fed Financial Stress Index 4 (STLFSI4) + VIX percentile + breadth + OI-PCR. Score composite 0-100. OBSERVATION (jaws-style, captures 4/7 corrections historiques).
Source: cockpit section #06-financial-stress, live data EOD 2026-05-15.
🎭 MEME IDEA: Two Buttons (sweating guy) — Two buttons: "Fed dovish surprise" and "Fed hawkish surprise." Trader sweating with both hedges and rotation playbook ready. · Caption: The macro trigger could come from anywhere. Have a playbook for both buttons.

The first event to watch is the Federal Reserve communication this week. There are no scheduled FOMC meetings, but there are several scheduled speeches from regional Fed presidents that will provide information about how the committee is thinking about the cycle. The market is currently pricing roughly fifty basis points of additional rate cuts through the end of the year. If the Fed communication this week pushes back against that pricing, the rate-sensitive sectors will respond.

Real estate, utilities, and the small-cap complex are the most sensitive. The interpretation depends on the direction of the surprise. A hawkish surprise reinforces the dispersion thesis because the mega-cap growth complex is less sensitive to rates than the broader market. A dovish surprise narrows the dispersion because the broader market participates in the rally.

The probability-weighted bias is hawkish given the consistent communication from the committee in recent weeks, but the path remains uncertain.

The second event is the earnings calendar. The companies reporting this week include several names in the cyclical and consumer discretionary space that will provide information about the strength of the underlying economy. The single most important earnings release this week is from a major retailer whose results will be read as a proxy for consumer spending strength. The reaction to that report will tell you whether the market is willing to weight cyclical exposure on the basis of weak data or strong data. The market reaction matters more than the absolute results, because reactions reveal positioning.

🎭 MEME IDEA: Drake Yes/No — Drake rejecting "What the earnings number says." Drake approving "How the market reacts to the earnings number." · Caption: Numbers don't move markets. Positioning does.

The third event is the options expiration cycle. We are approaching the May monthly expiration on the third Friday of the month. Monthly expirations have historically produced volatility in the days surrounding them, with the so-called window-dressing effects and dealer gamma rolloffs concentrating in the final week. The dealer gamma profile suggests that the upcoming expiration will see meaningful gamma rolloff on the mega-cap names.

🎭 MEME IDEA: ZeroHedge "OH NO" — Big red letters "Gamma rolloff incoming" over a calendar circled on May OPEX · Caption: Damping function thins out. Vol can pick up. Hedges sized accordingly.

The interpretation is that the damping effect of dealer hedging on the index will weaken in the week of expiration, and individual stock volatility may pick up. The hedges I am holding are sized to participate in any volatility spike that emerges in or after the expiration window.

The fourth event is the regular structural flow cycle. Mutual fund quarter-end window dressing typically occurs in the final two weeks of June, but the broader flow patterns of the second quarter are beginning to shape up. Pension fund rebalancing flows are expected to be modestly equity-positive this quarter, as the equity outperformance over fixed income year-to-date has pulled most diversified portfolios slightly underweight bonds. The rebalancing flow into fixed income should provide a modest tailwind to bonds and a modest headwind to equities. The magnitude is unlikely to be the deciding factor, but it adds a small probability tilt.

The fifth event is the broader macroeconomic data calendar. CPI is scheduled for release in the coming days. PCE will follow later in the month. The market is currently positioning for benign inflation prints, which is consistent with the equity-friendly environment that has prevailed.

09
9 vol indices · key ratios · master signal score
Volatility Dashboard — VIX / VIX9D / VIX3M / VIX6M / VXN / OVX / GVZ / VVIX / SKEW NEW

KEY RATIOS
VIX/VIX6M0.793——> 1.0 = backwardation = fear—🟢
VIX/VIX3M0.863——inverted = immediate fear—🟢
